phenomenon: Stellar Nucleosynthesis — How Stars Forge the Elements
key_measurement: The Sun's core temperature is 1.57 × 10⁷ K, at which the proton-proton (pp) chain dominates hydrogen fusion; more massive stars (Tc > ~1.8 × 10⁷ K) rely primarily on the CNO cycle — both processes produce the same net reaction (4H → He-4 + 2e⁺ + 2νe + 26.73 MeV), but CNO is ~10⁸× more temperature-sensitive. The iron peak (⁵⁶Fe, ⁵⁶Ni) represents the terminus of exothermic fusion — the binding energy per nucleon maximum at A ≈ 56 (Burbidge, Burbidge, Fowler & Hoyle 1957, Rev. Mod. Phys. 29, 547 — the 'B²FH' paper)
principle_set: Stellar nucleosynthesis is the creation of chemical elements by nuclear fusion reactions within stars; it has been occurring since the Big Bang produced the first hydrogen, helium, and lithium, and is responsible for all elements from carbon to iron in the periodic table

The proton-proton (pp) chain is the primary hydrogen-burning mechanism in stars with masses ≤ 1.3 solar masses (core T < ~1.8 × 10⁷ K): four protons fuse in a chain of steps to produce one helium-4 nucleus, releasing 26.73 MeV per reaction — this powers the Sun

The CNO (carbon-nitrogen-oxygen) cycle dominates hydrogen fusion in stars more massive than ~1.3 solar masses: carbon acts as a catalyst in a cyclic sequence (¹²C → ¹³N → ¹³C → ¹⁴N → ¹⁵O → ¹⁵N → ¹²C + ⁴He), producing helium-4 with the same net energy but with far higher temperature dependence (rate ∝ T²⁰)

After hydrogen exhaustion, stars with sufficient mass undergo sequential burning stages in onion-shell structure: helium burning (triple-alpha process: 3⁴He → ¹²C, then ¹²C + ⁴He → ¹⁶O), carbon burning, neon burning, oxygen burning, and silicon burning — each shell burning at higher temperatures and shorter timescales

Silicon burning culminates in the iron peak: ⁵⁶Fe (and nearby isotopes) has the highest nuclear binding energy per nucleon; fusing iron into heavier elements requires energy input rather than releasing it — this is why stars cannot fuse beyond iron and collapse when the iron core reaches the Chandrasekhar mass (~1.4 M☉)

Elements heavier than iron are produced by neutron capture processes: the s-process (slow neutron capture) occurs in AGB stars over thousands of years, producing elements up to lead and bismuth (e.g., Sr, Ba, Pb); the r-process (rapid neutron capture) requires extreme neutron fluxes in core-collapse supernovae and neutron star mergers, producing elements including gold, platinum, and uranium

The theoretical framework for stellar nucleosynthesis was established by the landmark B²FH paper (Burbidge, Burbidge, Fowler & Hoyle 1957, Rev. Mod. Phys. 29, 547), which identified eight distinct nucleosynthesis processes; William Fowler received the 1983 Nobel Prize in Physics for related work on nuclear reactions in stars

Supernova nucleosynthesis: in core-collapse supernovae (Type II), the explosive burning and neutrino-driven outflows produce and expel elements heavier than oxygen into the interstellar medium — the shock wave triggers explosive nucleosynthesis of nickel-56, cobalt-56, and iron-56, and the neutrino wind may also drive r-process nucleosynthesis (though neutron star mergers are now favored as the primary r-process site)
framework: B²FH theory of stellar nucleosynthesis (Burbidge, Burbidge, Fowler & Hoyle 1957); nuclear astrophysics; stellar evolution models; s-process and r-process nucleosynthesis theory
institutions: California Institute of Technology (Fowler; NACRE nuclear reaction rate compilations)
Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory (stellar evolution models)
Joint Institute for Nuclear Astrophysics – Center for the Evolution of the Elements (JINA-CEE)

sources.claim: Stellar nucleosynthesis theory established in landmark B²FH paper identifying eight nucleosynthesis processes including pp chain, CNO cycle, alpha processes, s-process, and r-process; Fred Hoyle first proposed stellar nucleosynthesis in 1946
sources.citation: Burbidge, Burbidge, Fowler & Hoyle 1957, Reviews of Modern Physics 29, 547 (the 'B²FH' paper)

sources.claim: The Sun's core temperature is 1.57 × 10⁷ K; at this temperature the pp chain dominates over the CNO cycle; combined energy generation in massive stars shows CNO dominates above ~1.8 × 10⁷ K
sources.citation: Wikipedia: Stellar nucleosynthesis (citing standard solar model results); Bahcall et al. 2005

sources.claim: The triple-alpha process (helium burning: 3⁴He → ¹²C) occurs in red giant cores at T ~ 10⁸ K; this is the fundamental mechanism for carbon production in the universe

The s-process (slow neutron capture) in AGB stars produces elements from strontium to bismuth; the r-process (rapid neutron capture) in neutron star mergers (confirmed by GW170817) and possibly supernovae produces elements heavier than bismuth including gold, platinum, uranium
sources.citation: Wikipedia: Stellar nucleosynthesis; Cowan et al. 2021, Rev. Mod. Phys. 93, 015002

sources.claim: Iron-56 represents the terminus of exothermic stellar fusion — it has the highest binding energy per nucleon of any nucleus (~8.8 MeV/nucleon); stars cannot generate energy by fusing iron, leading to core collapse
sources.citation: B²FH 1957; standard nuclear physics — binding energy per nucleon curve

visualization_notes: This subject is the backbone of the cosmic element-origin story — the forge inside stars. Two complementary visual tracks: MICROSCALE (nuclear reactions) and MACROSCALE (stellar evolution). Microscale visual: inside a stellar core — a sea of glowing plasma. Protons visualized as small luminous spheres being forced together by extreme pressure and temperature. PP chain sequence: two protons fuse → deuterium (with a positron and neutrino flashing away) → deuterium + proton → helium-3 → two helium-3 → helium-4 + two protons. Each step accompanied by a flash of gamma-ray energy. CNO cycle: visualize as a conveyor belt — carbon-12 nucleus acts as a catalyst, cycling through nitrogen and oxygen isotopes, each step adding a proton and emitting a positron or neutrino, finally returning to carbon-12 plus a helium-4. Macroscale visual 1 — Onion shell structure: cross-section of a massive star near end-of-life, showing concentric burning shells. From outside to center: hydrogen-burning shell (red), helium-burning shell (orange), carbon-burning shell (yellow), neon/oxygen shell (white-hot), silicon-burning shell (blue-white), and an iron core at center (dark, inert, deadly). Each shell gets hotter and burns faster — hydrogen burns for millions of years; silicon burns for days. Macroscale visual 2 — Core collapse supernova: when the iron core exceeds ~1.4 solar masses, gravity wins instantly. Collapse happens in milliseconds — the core rebounds as a neutron star, sending a shockwave outward through all the shells. The shockwave triggers explosive nucleosynthesis. The outer layers are ejected at ~10,000 km/s in a brilliant supernova explosion, seeding the surrounding interstellar medium with all the elements forged inside. JWST imagery available: NGC 3603 nebula (young star cluster), SN 1987A supernova remnant (publicly released by JWST 2023 — credit NASA/ESA/CSA). The Crab Nebula (Hubble/Chandra composite — public release). Most cinematically powerful moment: the cross-section reveal of the onion-shell structure of a dying massive star — then the millisecond collapse and rebound, followed by the supernova bloom. The contrast between the millions of years of hydrogen burning and the days of silicon burning is a powerful time-scale narrative device.
visualization_compliance: No classified data — all stellar nucleosynthesis research is open academic literature
JWST SN 1987A images (2023) publicly released — credit NASA/ESA/CSA/STScI required
Hubble/Chandra Crab Nebula images are publicly released — credit NASA/ESA/CXC
Artistic cross-section depictions of stellar interiors are standard in documentary production
B²FH paper (1957) is historical record — no rights issues; William Fowler Nobel Prize imagery available through Nobel Foundation
